title: Dnmt3a-mutated clonal hematopoiesis promotes osteoporosis
research_question: Does clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP), particularly Dnmt3a mutations, causally contribute to osteoporosis?
methods_summary: Analysis of UK Biobank exome sequencing data for association between CHIP and osteoporosis diagnoses and bone mineral density
Murine bone marrow transplantation models with hematopoietic-specific Dnmt3a or Tet2 knockout
Micro-computed tomography (µCT) for bone mass assessment
CRISPR-Cas9 genome-wide screen in RAW264.7 cells
Reduced representation bisulfite sequencing (RRBS), ATAC-seq, and ChIP-seq for epigenetic and transcriptional analysis
dataset: UK Biobank (113,641 unrelated individuals of European ancestry with whole-exome sequencing); murine models (Dnmt3afl/flVav1-Cre, Tet2fl/flVav1-Cre, Dnmt3aR878H)
key_findings: CHIP is associated with increased incident osteoporosis diagnoses (HR 1.44, 95% CI 1.22-1.72) and decreased estimated bone mineral density in humans
Hematopoietic-specific Dnmt3a mutations in mice decrease trabecular and cortical bone mass via increased osteoclastogenesis
Dnmt3a-mutant macrophages secrete proinflammatory cytokines, including IL-20, in a non-cell-autonomous manner to promote osteoclast differentiation
IL-20 neutralization or alendronate treatment reverses bone loss in Dnmt3a-mutant mice
Dnmt3a loss leads to hypomethylation, increased chromatin accessibility, and enhanced Irf3-NF-κB binding at the Il20 locus
extracted_insights: CHIP mutations, particularly in DNMT3A, causally promote osteoporosis through a non-cell-autonomous mechanism where mutant macrophages secrete IL-20 via Irf3-NF-κB signaling, driving osteoclastogenesis and bone loss, which can be therapeutically targeted by IL-20 neutralization or bisphosphonates.
